summary: Gruber revisits his May 2023 prediction that Bluesky would replace Twitter and achieve mainstream popularity, arguing that events have vindicated him. He contrasts Bluesky's intuitive, Twitter-like simplicity with Mastodon's nerd-friendly but confusing design, and Threads' soulless blandness. He reflects on the post-Twitter diaspora across Mastodon, Bluesky, Threads, and X, noting he spends less total time on social media now. Gruber argues Bluesky has become what Twitter once aspired to be — user-controlled, vibrant, and approachable — while acknowledging Mastodon remains his personal favorite for signal-to-noise ratio.
key_points: Gruber's 2023 prediction that Bluesky would succeed where Mastodon couldn't — by being simple, fun, and immediately understandable — appears to be proving correct
The post-Twitter landscape is now a diaspora across four platforms, each with distinct vibes, and Gruber believes he's better off spending less total time across all of them
Threads lacks a genuine vision beyond antagonizing Elon Musk, making it a platform without a soul that aspires to blandness
Bluesky's centralized design bothers federation purists but is exactly what makes it approachable and performant compared to Mastodon
Mediocre first-party apps for Bluesky and Threads paradoxically help by limiting time spent on those platforms
money_quotes: Bluesky is going to skyrocket to mainstream popularity and actually replace Twitter, and Mastodon cannot, because Bluesky is being designed to be simple, fun, and — most importantly — easy to understand.
What makes Mastodon good for us nerds is that all the non-nerds aren't here.

It's a platform without a soul. It aspires to be anodyne, which is very different than empowering users not to feel like they've got to dodge a never-ending barrage of turds being thrown by the angry chaos monkeys who've overrun X.
If Threads does have a vibe, that vibe is blandness.
Shitbirds get blocked and added to blocklists, not elevated to the top of reply threads because they paid for a blue-check power-up.
The centralized nature of the Bluesky platform gives the hardcore federation zealots the heebie-jeebies, but that's what makes Bluesky understandable and approachable.
I'm pretty sure I spend less time overall using all of them combined today than I did for Twitter's peak decade-or-so, and that I'm better off for that.
key_insight: Bluesky succeeded where Mastodon couldn't because it prioritized being simple and familiar over being technically pure, proving that mainstream social platforms win by putting user experience first.
